revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
[[File:Guji Francesco Brigante.jpg|thumb|A Shinto rite carried out at a jinja in {{ill|San Marino|en|San Marino|WD=Q238|lt=San Marino}}, Southern Europe]]
'''Overseas Shinto''' designates the practice of the Japanese religion of [[Shinto]] outside Japan itself. Shinto has spread abroad by various methods, including the imperial expansion of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the migration of Japanese to other countries, and the embrace of Shinto by various non-Japanese individuals.

{{lang|ja-Latn|Jinja}} outside Japan are termed {{lang|ja-Latn|kaigai jinja}} ("overseas shrines"), a term coined by {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}}.{{sfn|Suga|2010|p=48}} 

== In Europe ==
On June 22, 2014, Europe's first official Shinto Shrine: {{ill|San Marino Shrine|en|San Marino Shrine|WD=Q21192776|lt=San Marino Shrine}} was opened in [[W:Serravalle, San Marino|Serravalle, San Marino]]. The San Marino shrine was inaugurated in the presence of 150 personalities including the president of the Association of Shinto Shrines and Yoko Kishi, mother of the former Prime Minister of Japan {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzō Abe}} and daughter of Prime Minister {{ill|Nobusuke Kishi|en|Nobusuke Kishi|WD=Q315572|lt=Nobusuke Kishi}}.<ref>[https://giornalesm.com/266285/ San Marino. Sarà inaugurato questa mattina alle 10.30 al podere Lesignano, il San Marino Jinja]</ref><ref>{{cite web |lang = it |title=Un tempio shintoista a San Marino. E' il primo ufficiale in Europa|website=Il resto del Carlino |date=2014-06-22|url=https://www.ilrestodelcarlino.it/rimini/cronaca/2014/06/22/1082095-san-marino-tempio-shintoista.shtml}}</ref> The shrine was established in commemoration of the victims of the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}.<ref>{{cite web |lang = it |url = https://st.ilsole24ore.com/art/notizie/2014-05-25/shintoisti-san-marino-081510.shtml |title = Shintoisti a San Marino |author = Stefano Carrer |date = 2014-05-25 |website = Il Sole 24 ORE |access-date = 2023-09-13}}</ref>

==In Asia==
[[File:Chosen Jingu.JPG|thumb|The [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|WD=Q8684|lt=Seoul}}, Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula]]
In Japanese-controlled territories ({{ill|Wang Jingwei regime|en|Wang Jingwei regime|WD=Q696242|lt=China}}, {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea}}, the {{ill|South Seas Mandate|en|South Seas Mandate|WD=Q718763|lt=Pacific Islands}}, [[:en:Singapore under Japanese rule|Singapore]], {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Taiwan}}, {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} and {{ill|Karafuto Prefecture|en|Karafuto Prefecture|WD=Q696736|lt=Karafuto Prefecture}}), shrines were erected by both Japanese settlers and colonial authorities. Most of the sanctuaries were built by settlers, but many of them later received government recognition and funding. At first, ''jinja'' built Japanese communities for their use, often using ritual objects from their old shrines.{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=22}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=30—32}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}} In 1900, the Japanese authorities erected the first large shrine in the colonies, it became [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]]. It was followed by other major shrines, such as {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}} in Korea and {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|en|Karafuto Shrine|WD=Q11543654|lt=Karafuto Shrine}} on Sakhalin. These shrines were built by decree of the governor and without consultation with the Shinto clergy, and their policies were determined by the military.{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=404—406}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=36}}

==In the Americas==

===United States===
The first Shinto shrine in Hawaii was {{ill|Hilo Daijingu|ja|ヒロ大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hilo Daijingu|qq=}}, built in 1898.<ref>{{cite journal |url = https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498 |author = Shimizu, Karli |title = Religion and Secularism in Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Case Study on Hilo Daijingū, 1898–1941 |year = 2019 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume = 46 |issue = 1 |pages = 1–30 |jstor = 26854498 |issn = 0304-1042 |archivedate = 2021-05-04 |archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20210504095834/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498}}</ref> It is known that shortly before the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|WD=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor}} on December 7, 1941, Japanese Shintoists in Hawaii held ceremonies in honor of the former commander-in-chief of the Japanese fleet, [[Tōgō Heihachirō]]. One of the shrines, built at the beginning of the 20th century, was subordinate to both the Japanese authorities responsible for the shrine system and the American authorities, since it was registered in the state as a non-profit organization. American Shintoists were culturally influenced by their local environment. Thus, the spirits of {{ill|George Washington|en|George Washington|WD=Q23|lt=George Washington}} and Hawaiian King {{ill|Kamehameha I|en|Kamehameha I|WD=Q214910|lt=Kamehameha I}} entered the local pantheon. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the American government closed the shrine, fearing the growth of anti-American sentiment among the Japanese, but after the surrender of Japan in September 1945, the shrine continued its work. The few shrines on the West Coast of the United States were not restored after the war.<ref>{{cite journal |last = Hansen |first =Wilburn |title = Examining Prewar Togo Worship in Hawaii Toward Rethinking Hawaiian Shinto as a New Religion in America |year = 2010 |journal = Nova Religio |volume = 14 |number = 1 |pages = 67–92 |issn = 1092-6690 |doi = 10.1525/nr.2010.14.1.67}}</ref><ref name="Abe_Imamura2019">{{cite journal |author    = Abe DK, Imamura A |title = The destruction of Shinto shrines in Hawaii and the West Coast during World War II: the lingering effects of Pearl Harbor and Japanese-American internment |url   = https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816 |journal = Asian Anthropology  |year = 2019  |volume=18  |number= 4 |pages = 266–281 |doi = 10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816}}</ref>

===Brazil===
Two Shinto shrines were built in Brazil before the outbreak of World War II, both in the {{ill|São Paulo (state)|en|São Paulo (state)|WD=Q175|lt=state of São Paulo}}. The first one was built in 1920 by Uetsuka Shuhei in the city of {{ill|Promissão|en|Promissão|WD=Q431377|lt=Promissão}}. The second temple was built by immigrants in the municipality of {{ill|Bastos|en|Bastos|WD=Q4868368|lt=Bastos}} 18 years later. Most emigrants performed rituals of worshiping the gods with their families, and less often with their neighbors.<ref>{{cite journal |author = FRank Usarski, Rafael Shoji |title = Buddhism, Shinto and Japanese New Religions in Brazil |year = 2017 |journal = Handbook of Contemporary Religions in Brazil |pages = 279–294 |doi = 10.1163/9789004322134_018|isbn = 9789004322134 }}</ref> 11 major shrines are still located in Brazil.{{sfn|Picken|2011|p=274}}

== In Oceania ==

=== Micronesia ===
{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} dating from during or after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} exist in some {{ill|Micronesia|en|Micronesia|WD=Q3359409|lt=Micronesian}} countries.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oskow |first=Noah |date=2020-07-13 |title=When Japan Ruled the Waves: The Forgotten Colonies of Micronesia |url=https://unseen-japan.com/japan-forgotten-colonies-micronesia/ |access-date=2023-09-24 |website=Unseen Japan |language=en-US}}</ref>

== In Africa ==

=== Sub-Saharan Africa ===
Some Shinto-inspired new religions have gained ground in various cities in {{ill|Sub-Saharan Africa|en|Sub-Saharan Africa|WD=Q132959|lt=Sub-Saharan Africa}}.<ref>{{Citation |last=Louveau |first=Frédérique |title=Japanese Spiritualities in Africa: From a Transnational Space to the Creation of a Local Lifestyle |date=2020-07-06 |work=Transnational Religious Spaces |pages= |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |access-date=2024-01-25 |place=[[Munich]] |publisher=[[De Gruyter]] |language=en |doi=10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |isbn=978-3-11-069010-1}}</ref>

==References==
===Citations===
{{Reflist|20em}}

===Sources===
{{Refbegin|30em|indent=yes}}
* {{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |year=2004 |title=Japanese Religion: Unity and Diversity |edition=fourth |location=Belmont, CA |publisher=Wadsworth |isbn=978-0-534-17694-5 }}
* {{cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |location=Oxford |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2017 |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 }}
* {{cite journal |last = Nakajima |first = Michio |title = Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |year = 2010 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |doi = 10.18874/jjrs.37.1.2010.21-46|doi-access = free }}
* {{cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |author-link=Stuart D. B. Picken |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |edition=second |location=Lanham |publisher=Scarecrow Press |year=2011 |isbn=978-0-8108-7172-4 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Suga |first=Kōji |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |year=2010 |pages=47–74 }}
{{Refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
[[File:Guji Francesco Brigante.jpg|thumb|A Shinto rite carried out at a jinja in {{ill|San Marino|en|San Marino|WD=Q238|lt=San Marino}}, Southern Europe]]
'''Overseas Shinto''' designates the practice of the Japanese religion of [[Shinto]] outside Japan itself. Shinto has spread abroad by various methods, including the imperial expansion of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the migration of Japanese to other countries, and the embrace of Shinto by various non-Japanese individuals.

{{lang|ja-Latn|Jinja}} outside Japan are termed {{lang|ja-Latn|kaigai jinja}} ("overseas shrines"), a term coined by {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}}.{{sfn|Suga|2010|p=48}} 

== In Europe ==
On June 22, 2014, Europe's first official Shinto Shrine: {{ill|San Marino Shrine|en|San Marino Shrine|WD=Q21192776|lt=San Marino Shrine}} was opened in [[W:Serravalle, San Marino|Serravalle, San Marino]]. The San Marino shrine was inaugurated in the presence of 150 personalities including the president of the Association of Shinto Shrines and Yoko Kishi, mother of the former Prime Minister of Japan {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzō Abe}} and daughter of Prime Minister {{ill|Nobusuke Kishi|en|Nobusuke Kishi|WD=Q315572|lt=Nobusuke Kishi}}.<ref>[https://giornalesm.com/266285/ San Marino. Sarà inaugurato questa mattina alle 10.30 al podere Lesignano, il San Marino Jinja]</ref><ref>{{cite web |lang = it |title=Un tempio shintoista a San Marino. E' il primo ufficiale in Europa|website=Il resto del Carlino |date=2014-06-22|url=https://www.ilrestodelcarlino.it/rimini/cronaca/2014/06/22/1082095-san-marino-tempio-shintoista.shtml}}</ref> The shrine was established in commemoration of the victims of the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}.<ref>{{cite web |lang = it |url = https://st.ilsole24ore.com/art/notizie/2014-05-25/shintoisti-san-marino-081510.shtml |title = Shintoisti a San Marino |author = Stefano Carrer |date = 2014-05-25 |website = Il Sole 24 ORE |access-date = 2023-09-13}}</ref>

==In Asia==
[[File:Chosen Jingu.JPG|thumb|The [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|WD=Q8684|lt=Seoul}}, Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula]]
In Japanese-controlled territories ({{ill|Wang Jingwei regime|en|Wang Jingwei regime|WD=Q696242|lt=China}}, {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea}}, the {{ill|South Seas Mandate|en|South Seas Mandate|WD=Q718763|lt=Pacific Islands}}, [[:en:Singapore under Japanese rule|Singapore]], {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Taiwan}}, {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} and {{ill|Karafuto Prefecture|en|Karafuto Prefecture|WD=Q696736|lt=Karafuto Prefecture}}), shrines were erected by both Japanese settlers and colonial authorities. Most of the sanctuaries were built by settlers, but many of them later received government recognition and funding. At first, ''jinja'' built Japanese communities for their use, often using ritual objects from their old shrines.{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=22}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=30—32}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}} In 1900, the Japanese authorities erected the first large shrine in the colonies, it became [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]]. It was followed by other major shrines, such as {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}} in Korea and {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|en|Karafuto Shrine|WD=Q11543654|lt=Karafuto Shrine}} on Sakhalin. These shrines were built by decree of the governor and without consultation with the Shinto clergy, and their policies were determined by the military.{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=404—406}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=36}}

==In the Americas==

===United States===
The first Shinto shrine in Hawaii was {{ill|Hilo Daijingu|ja|ヒロ大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hilo Daijingu|qq=}}, built in 1898.<ref>{{cite journal |url = https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498 |author = Shimizu, Karli |title = Religion and Secularism in Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Case Study on Hilo Daijingū, 1898–1941 |year = 2019 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume = 46 |issue = 1 |pages = 1–30 |jstor = 26854498 |issn = 0304-1042 |archivedate = 2021-05-04 |archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20210504095834/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498}}</ref> It is known that shortly before the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|WD=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor}} on December 7, 1941, Japanese Shintoists in Hawaii held ceremonies in honor of the former commander-in-chief of the Japanese fleet, [[Tōgō Heihachirō]]. One of the shrines, built at the beginning of the 20th century, was subordinate to both the Japanese authorities responsible for the shrine system and the American authorities, since it was registered in the state as a non-profit organization. American Shintoists were culturally influenced by their local environment. Thus, the spirits of {{ill|George Washington|en|George Washington|WD=Q23|lt=George Washington}} and Hawaiian King {{ill|Kamehameha I|en|Kamehameha I|WD=Q214910|lt=Kamehameha I}} entered the local pantheon. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the American government closed the shrine, fearing the growth of anti-American sentiment among the Japanese, but after the surrender of Japan in September 1945, the shrine continued its work. The few shrines on the West Coast of the United States were not restored after the war.<ref>{{cite journal |last = Hansen |first =Wilburn |title = Examining Prewar Togo Worship in Hawaii Toward Rethinking Hawaiian Shinto as a New Religion in America |year = 2010 |journal = Nova Religio |volume = 14 |number = 1 |pages = 67–92 |issn = 1092-6690 |doi = 10.1525/nr.2010.14.1.67}}</ref><ref name="Abe_Imamura2019">{{cite journal |author    = Abe DK, Imamura A |title = The destruction of Shinto shrines in Hawaii and the West Coast during World War II: the lingering effects of Pearl Harbor and Japanese-American internment |url   = https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816 |journal = Asian Anthropology  |year = 2019  |volume=18  |number= 4 |pages = 266–281 |doi = 10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816}}</ref>

===Brazil===
Two Shinto shrines were built in Brazil before the outbreak of World War II, both in the {{ill|São Paulo (state)|en|São Paulo (state)|WD=Q175|lt=state of São Paulo}}. The first one was built in 1920 by Uetsuka Shuhei in the city of {{ill|Promissão|en|Promissão|WD=Q431377|lt=Promissão}}. The second temple was built by immigrants in the municipality of {{ill|Bastos|en|Bastos|WD=Q4868368|lt=Bastos}} 18 years later. Most emigrants performed rituals of worshiping the gods with their families, and less often with their neighbors.<ref>{{cite journal |author = FRank Usarski, Rafael Shoji |title = Buddhism, Shinto and Japanese New Religions in Brazil |year = 2017 |journal = Handbook of Contemporary Religions in Brazil |pages = 279–294 |doi = 10.1163/9789004322134_018|isbn = 9789004322134 }}</ref> 11 major shrines are still located in Brazil.{{sfn|Picken|2011|p=274}}

== In Oceania ==

=== Micronesia ===
{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} dating from during or after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} exist in some {{ill|Micronesia|en|Micronesia|WD=Q3359409|lt=Micronesian}} countries.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oskow |first=Noah |date=2020-07-13 |title=When Japan Ruled the Waves: The Forgotten Colonies of Micronesia |url=https://unseen-japan.com/japan-forgotten-colonies-micronesia/ |access-date=2023-09-24 |website=Unseen Japan |language=en-US}}</ref>

== In Africa ==

=== Sub-Saharan Africa ===
Some Shinto-inspired new religions have gained ground in various cities in {{ill|Sub-Saharan Africa|en|Sub-Saharan Africa|WD=Q132959|lt=Sub-Saharan Africa}}.<ref>{{Citation |last=Louveau |first=Frédérique |title=Japanese Spiritualities in Africa: From a Transnational Space to the Creation of a Local Lifestyle |date=2020-07-06 |work=Transnational Religious Spaces |pages= |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |access-date=2024-01-25 |place=[[Munich]] |publisher=[[De Gruyter]] |language=en |doi=10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |isbn=978-3-11-069010-1}}</ref>

==References==
===Citations===

===Sources===

* {{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |year=2004 |title=Japanese Religion: Unity and Diversity |edition=fourth |location=Belmont, CA |publisher=Wadsworth |isbn=978-0-534-17694-5 }}

* {{cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |author-link=Stuart D. B. Picken |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |edition=second |location=Lanham |publisher=Scarecrow Press |year=2011 |isbn=978-0-8108-7172-4 }}

Shinto has spread abroad by various methods, including the imperial expansion of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the migration of Japanese to other countries, and the embrace of Shinto by various non-Japanese individuals.

{{lang|ja-Latn|Jinja}} outside Japan are termed {{lang|ja-Latn|kaigai jinja}} ("overseas shrines"), a term coined by {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}}.{{sfn|Suga|2010|p=48}}

On June 22, 2014, Europe's first official Shinto Shrine: {{ill|San Marino Shrine|en|San Marino Shrine|WD=Q21192776|lt=San Marino Shrine}} was opened in [[W:Serravalle, San Marino|Serravalle, San Marino]]. The San Marino shrine was inaugurated in the presence of 150 personalities including the president of the Association of Shinto Shrines and Yoko Kishi, mother of the former Prime Minister of Japan {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzō Abe}} and daughter of Prime Minister {{ill|Nobusuke Kishi|en|Nobusuke Kishi|WD=Q315572|lt=Nobusuke Kishi}}.<ref>[https://giornalesm.com/266285/ San Marino. Sarà inaugurato questa mattina alle 10.30 al podere Lesignano, il San Marino Jinja]</ref><ref>{{cite web |lang = it |title=Un tempio shintoista a San Marino. E' il primo ufficiale in Europa|website=Il resto del Carlino |date=2014-06-22|url=https://www.ilrestodelcarlino.it/rimini/cronaca/2014/06/22/1082095-san-marino-tempio-shintoista.shtml}}</ref> The shrine was established in commemoration of the victims of the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}.<ref>{{cite web |lang = it |url = https://st.ilsole24ore.com/art/notizie/2014-05-25/shintoisti-san-marino-081510.shtml |title = Shintoisti a San Marino |author = Stefano Carrer |date = 2014-05-25 |website = Il Sole 24 ORE |access-date = 2023-09-13}}</ref>

==In Asia==
[[File:Chosen Jingu.JPG|thumb|The [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|WD=Q8684|lt=Seoul}}, Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula]]
In Japanese-controlled territories ({{ill|Wang Jingwei regime|en|Wang Jingwei regime|WD=Q696242|lt=China}}, {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea}}, the {{ill|South Seas Mandate|en|South Seas Mandate|WD=Q718763|lt=Pacific Islands}}, [[:en:Singapore under Japanese rule|Singapore]], {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Taiwan}}, {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo}} and {{ill|Karafuto Prefecture|en|Karafuto Prefecture|WD=Q696736|lt=Karafuto Prefecture}}), shrines were erected by both Japanese settlers and colonial authorities. Most of the sanctuaries were built by settlers, but many of them later received government recognition and funding. At first, ''jinja'' built Japanese communities for their use, often using ritual objects from their old shrines.{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=22}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=30—32}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}} In 1900, the Japanese authorities erected the first large shrine in the colonies, it became [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]]. It was followed by other major shrines, such as {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine}} in Korea and {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|en|Karafuto Shrine|WD=Q11543654|lt=Karafuto Shrine}} on Sakhalin. These shrines were built by decree of the governor and without consultation with the Shinto clergy, and their policies were determined by the military.{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=404—406}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=36}}

==In the Americas==

===United States===
The first Shinto shrine in Hawaii was {{ill|Hilo Daijingu|ja|ヒロ大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hilo Daijingu|qq=}}, built in 1898.<ref>{{cite journal |url = https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498 |author = Shimizu, Karli |title = Religion and Secularism in Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Case Study on Hilo Daijingū, 1898–1941 |year = 2019 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume = 46 |issue = 1 |pages = 1–30 |jstor = 26854498 |issn = 0304-1042 |archivedate = 2021-05-04 |archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20210504095834/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498}}</ref> It is known that shortly before the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|WD=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor}} on December 7, 1941, Japanese Shintoists in Hawaii held ceremonies in honor of the former commander-in-chief of the Japanese fleet, [[Tōgō Heihachirō]]. One of the shrines, built at the beginning of the 20th century, was subordinate to both the Japanese authorities responsible for the shrine system and the American authorities, since it was registered in the state as a non-profit organization. American Shintoists were culturally influenced by their local environment. Thus, the spirits of {{ill|George Washington|en|George Washington|WD=Q23|lt=George Washington}} and Hawaiian King {{ill|Kamehameha I|en|Kamehameha I|WD=Q214910|lt=Kamehameha I}} entered the local pantheon. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the American government closed the shrine, fearing the growth of anti-American sentiment among the Japanese, but after the surrender of Japan in September 1945, the shrine continued its work. The few shrines on the West Coast of the United States were not restored after the war.<ref>{{cite journal |last = Hansen |first =Wilburn |title = Examining Prewar Togo Worship in Hawaii Toward Rethinking Hawaiian Shinto as a New Religion in America |year = 2010 |journal = Nova Religio |volume = 14 |number = 1 |pages = 67–92 |issn = 1092-6690 |doi = 10.1525/nr.2010.14.1.67}}</ref><ref name="Abe_Imamura2019">{{cite journal |author    = Abe DK, Imamura A |title = The destruction of Shinto shrines in Hawaii and the West Coast during World War II: the lingering effects of Pearl Harbor and Japanese-American internment |url   = https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816 |journal = Asian Anthropology  |year = 2019  |volume=18  |number= 4 |pages = 266–281 |doi = 10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816}}</ref>

===Brazil===
Two Shinto shrines were built in Brazil before the outbreak of World War II, both in the {{ill|São Paulo (state)|en|São Paulo (state)|WD=Q175|lt=state of São Paulo}}. The first one was built in 1920 by Uetsuka Shuhei in the city of {{ill|Promissão|en|Promissão|WD=Q431377|lt=Promissão}}. The second temple was built by immigrants in the municipality of {{ill|Bastos|en|Bastos|WD=Q4868368|lt=Bastos}} 18 years later. Most emigrants performed rituals of worshiping the gods with their families, and less often with their neighbors.<ref>{{cite journal |author = FRank Usarski, Rafael Shoji |title = Buddhism, Shinto and Japanese New Religions in Brazil |year = 2017 |journal = Handbook of Contemporary Religions in Brazil |pages = 279–294 |doi = 10.1163/9789004322134_018|isbn = 9789004322134 }}</ref> 11 major shrines are still located in Brazil.{{sfn|Picken|2011|p=274}}

== In Oceania ==

=== Micronesia ===
{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines}} dating from during or after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}} exist in some {{ill|Micronesia|en|Micronesia|WD=Q3359409|lt=Micronesian}} countries.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oskow |first=Noah |date=2020-07-13 |title=When Japan Ruled the Waves: The Forgotten Colonies of Micronesia |url=https://unseen-japan.com/japan-forgotten-colonies-micronesia/ |access-date=2023-09-24 |website=Unseen Japan |language=en-US}}</ref>

== In Africa ==

=== Sub-Saharan Africa ===
Some Shinto-inspired new religions have gained ground in various cities in {{ill|Sub-Saharan Africa|en|Sub-Saharan Africa|WD=Q132959|lt=Sub-Saharan Africa}}.<ref>{{Citation |last=Louveau |first=Frédérique |title=Japanese Spiritualities in Africa: From a Transnational Space to the Creation of a Local Lifestyle |date=2020-07-06 |work=Transnational Religious Spaces |pages= |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |access-date=2024-01-25 |place=[[Munich]] |publisher=[[De Gruyter]] |language=en |doi=10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |isbn=978-3-11-069010-1}}</ref>

===Sources===

* {{cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |location=Oxford |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2017 |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 }}

* {{cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |author-link=Stuart D. B. Picken |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |edition=second |location=Lanham |publisher=Scarecrow Press |year=2011 |isbn=978-0-8108-7172-4 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Suga |first=Kōji |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |year=2010 |pages=47–74 }}
{{Refend}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
[[File:Guji Francesco Brigante.jpg|thumb|A Shinto rite carried out at a jinja in {{ill|San Marino|en|San Marino|WD=Q238|lt=San Marino|1=San Marino}}, Southern Europe]]
'''Overseas Shinto''' designates the practice of the Japanese religion of [[Shinto]] outside Japan itself. Shinto has spread abroad by various methods, including the imperial expansion of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the migration of Japanese to other countries, and the embrace of Shinto by various non-Japanese individuals.

{{lang|ja-Latn|Jinja}} outside Japan are termed {{lang|ja-Latn|kaigai jinja}} ("overseas shrines"), a term coined by {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}}.{{sfn|Suga|2010|p=48}} 

== In Europe ==
On June 22, 2014, Europe's first official Shinto Shrine: {{ill|San Marino Shrine|en|San Marino Shrine|WD=Q21192776|lt=San Marino Shrine|1=San Marino Shrine}} was opened in [[W:Serravalle, San Marino|Serravalle, San Marino]]. The San Marino shrine was inaugurated in the presence of 150 personalities including the president of the Association of Shinto Shrines and Yoko Kishi, mother of the former Prime Minister of Japan {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzō Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} and daughter of Prime Minister {{ill|Nobusuke Kishi|en|Nobusuke Kishi|WD=Q315572|lt=Nobusuke Kishi|1=Nobusuke Kishi}}.<ref>[https://giornalesm.com/266285/ San Marino. Sarà inaugurato questa mattina alle 10.30 al podere Lesignano, il San Marino Jinja]</ref><ref>{{cite web |lang = it |title=Un tempio shintoista a San Marino. E' il primo ufficiale in Europa|website=Il resto del Carlino |date=2014-06-22|url=https://www.ilrestodelcarlino.it/rimini/cronaca/2014/06/22/1082095-san-marino-tempio-shintoista.shtml}}</ref> The shrine was established in commemoration of the victims of the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}.<ref>{{cite web |lang = it |url = https://st.ilsole24ore.com/art/notizie/2014-05-25/shintoisti-san-marino-081510.shtml |title = Shintoisti a San Marino |author = Stefano Carrer |date = 2014-05-25 |website = Il Sole 24 ORE |access-date = 2023-09-13}}</ref>

==In Asia==
[[File:Chosen Jingu.JPG|thumb|The [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|WD=Q8684|lt=Seoul|1=Seoul}}, Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula]]
In Japanese-controlled territories ({{ill|Wang Jingwei regime|en|Wang Jingwei regime|WD=Q696242|lt=China|1=Wang Jingwei regime}}, {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea|1=Korea under Japanese rule}}, the {{ill|South Seas Mandate|en|South Seas Mandate|WD=Q718763|lt=Pacific Islands|1=South Seas Mandate}}, [[:en:Singapore under Japanese rule|Singapore]], {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}, {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} and {{ill|Karafuto Prefecture|en|Karafuto Prefecture|WD=Q696736|lt=Karafuto Prefecture|1=Karafuto Prefecture}}), shrines were erected by both Japanese settlers and colonial authorities. Most of the sanctuaries were built by settlers, but many of them later received government recognition and funding. At first, ''jinja'' built Japanese communities for their use, often using ritual objects from their old shrines.{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=22}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=30—32}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}} In 1900, the Japanese authorities erected the first large shrine in the colonies, it became [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]]. It was followed by other major shrines, such as {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}} in Korea and {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|en|Karafuto Shrine|WD=Q11543654|lt=Karafuto Shrine|1=Karafuto Shrine}} on Sakhalin. These shrines were built by decree of the governor and without consultation with the Shinto clergy, and their policies were determined by the military.{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=404—406}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=36}}

==In the Americas==

===United States===
The first Shinto shrine in Hawaii was {{ill|Hilo Daijingu|ja|ヒロ大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hilo Daijingu|qq=}}, built in 1898.<ref>{{cite journal |url = https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498 |author = Shimizu, Karli |title = Religion and Secularism in Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Case Study on Hilo Daijingū, 1898–1941 |year = 2019 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume = 46 |issue = 1 |pages = 1–30 |jstor = 26854498 |issn = 0304-1042 |archivedate = 2021-05-04 |archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20210504095834/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498}}</ref> It is known that shortly before the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|WD=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor|1=Attack on Pearl Harbor}} on December 7, 1941, Japanese Shintoists in Hawaii held ceremonies in honor of the former commander-in-chief of the Japanese fleet, [[Tōgō Heihachirō]]. One of the shrines, built at the beginning of the 20th century, was subordinate to both the Japanese authorities responsible for the shrine system and the American authorities, since it was registered in the state as a non-profit organization. American Shintoists were culturally influenced by their local environment. Thus, the spirits of {{ill|George Washington|en|George Washington|WD=Q23|lt=George Washington|1=George Washington}} and Hawaiian King {{ill|Kamehameha I|en|Kamehameha I|WD=Q214910|lt=Kamehameha I|1=Kamehameha I}} entered the local pantheon. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the American government closed the shrine, fearing the growth of anti-American sentiment among the Japanese, but after the surrender of Japan in September 1945, the shrine continued its work. The few shrines on the West Coast of the United States were not restored after the war.<ref>{{cite journal |last = Hansen |first =Wilburn |title = Examining Prewar Togo Worship in Hawaii Toward Rethinking Hawaiian Shinto as a New Religion in America |year = 2010 |journal = Nova Religio |volume = 14 |number = 1 |pages = 67–92 |issn = 1092-6690 |doi = 10.1525/nr.2010.14.1.67}}</ref><ref name="Abe_Imamura2019">{{cite journal |author    = Abe DK, Imamura A |title = The destruction of Shinto shrines in Hawaii and the West Coast during World War II: the lingering effects of Pearl Harbor and Japanese-American internment |url   = https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816 |journal = Asian Anthropology  |year = 2019  |volume=18  |number= 4 |pages = 266–281 |doi = 10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816}}</ref>

===Brazil===
Two Shinto shrines were built in Brazil before the outbreak of World War II, both in the {{ill|São Paulo (state)|en|São Paulo (state)|WD=Q175|lt=state of São Paulo|1=São Paulo (state)}}. The first one was built in 1920 by Uetsuka Shuhei in the city of {{ill|Promissão|en|Promissão|WD=Q431377|lt=Promissão|1=Promissão}}. The second temple was built by immigrants in the municipality of {{ill|Bastos|en|Bastos|WD=Q4868368|lt=Bastos|1=Bastos}} 18 years later. Most emigrants performed rituals of worshiping the gods with their families, and less often with their neighbors.<ref>{{cite journal |author = FRank Usarski, Rafael Shoji |title = Buddhism, Shinto and Japanese New Religions in Brazil |year = 2017 |journal = Handbook of Contemporary Religions in Brazil |pages = 279–294 |doi = 10.1163/9789004322134_018|isbn = 9789004322134 }}</ref> 11 major shrines are still located in Brazil.{{sfn|Picken|2011|p=274}}

== In Oceania ==

=== Micronesia ===
{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} dating from during or after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} exist in some {{ill|Micronesia|en|Micronesia|WD=Q3359409|lt=Micronesian|1=Micronesia}} countries.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oskow |first=Noah |date=2020-07-13 |title=When Japan Ruled the Waves: The Forgotten Colonies of Micronesia |url=https://unseen-japan.com/japan-forgotten-colonies-micronesia/ |access-date=2023-09-24 |website=Unseen Japan |language=en-US}}</ref>

== In Africa ==

=== Sub-Saharan Africa ===
Some Shinto-inspired new religions have gained ground in various cities in {{ill|Sub-Saharan Africa|en|Sub-Saharan Africa|WD=Q132959|lt=Sub-Saharan Africa|1=Sub-Saharan Africa}}.<ref>{{Citation |last=Louveau |first=Frédérique |title=Japanese Spiritualities in Africa: From a Transnational Space to the Creation of a Local Lifestyle |date=2020-07-06 |work=Transnational Religious Spaces |pages= |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |access-date=2024-01-25 |place=[[Munich]] |publisher=[[De Gruyter]] |language=en |doi=10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |isbn=978-3-11-069010-1}}</ref>

[[File:Chosen Jingu.JPG|thumb|The [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|WD=Q8684|lt=Seoul|1=Seoul}}, Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula]]

It was followed by other major shrines, such as {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}} in Korea and {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|en|Karafuto Shrine|WD=Q11543654|lt=Karafuto Shrine|1=Karafuto Shrine}} on Sakhalin. These shrines were built by decree of the governor and without consultation with the Shinto clergy, and their policies were determined by the military.{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=404—406}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=36}}

==In the Americas==

===United States===
The first Shinto shrine in Hawaii was {{ill|Hilo Daijingu|ja|ヒロ大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hilo Daijingu|qq=}}, built in 1898.<ref>{{cite journal |url = https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498 |author = Shimizu, Karli |title = Religion and Secularism in Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Case Study on Hilo Daijingū, 1898–1941 |year = 2019 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume = 46 |issue = 1 |pages = 1–30 |jstor = 26854498 |issn = 0304-1042 |archivedate = 2021-05-04 |archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20210504095834/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498}}</ref> It is known that shortly before the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|WD=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor|1=Attack on Pearl Harbor}} on December 7, 1941, Japanese Shintoists in Hawaii held ceremonies in honor of the former commander-in-chief of the Japanese fleet, [[Tōgō Heihachirō]]. One of the shrines, built at the beginning of the 20th century, was subordinate to both the Japanese authorities responsible for the shrine system and the American authorities, since it was registered in the state as a non-profit organization. American Shintoists were culturally influenced by their local environment. Thus, the spirits of {{ill|George Washington|en|George Washington|WD=Q23|lt=George Washington|1=George Washington}} and Hawaiian King {{ill|Kamehameha I|en|Kamehameha I|WD=Q214910|lt=Kamehameha I|1=Kamehameha I}} entered the local pantheon. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the American government closed the shrine, fearing the growth of anti-American sentiment among the Japanese, but after the surrender of Japan in September 1945, the shrine continued its work. The few shrines on the West Coast of the United States were not restored after the war.<ref>{{cite journal |last = Hansen |first =Wilburn |title = Examining Prewar Togo Worship in Hawaii Toward Rethinking Hawaiian Shinto as a New Religion in America |year = 2010 |journal = Nova Religio |volume = 14 |number = 1 |pages = 67–92 |issn = 1092-6690 |doi = 10.1525/nr.2010.14.1.67}}</ref><ref name="Abe_Imamura2019">{{cite journal |author    = Abe DK, Imamura A |title = The destruction of Shinto shrines in Hawaii and the West Coast during World War II: the lingering effects of Pearl Harbor and Japanese-American internment |url   = https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816 |journal = Asian Anthropology  |year = 2019  |volume=18  |number= 4 |pages = 266–281 |doi = 10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816}}</ref>

===Brazil===
Two Shinto shrines were built in Brazil before the outbreak of World War II, both in the {{ill|São Paulo (state)|en|São Paulo (state)|WD=Q175|lt=state of São Paulo|1=São Paulo (state)}}. The first one was built in 1920 by Uetsuka Shuhei in the city of {{ill|Promissão|en|Promissão|WD=Q431377|lt=Promissão|1=Promissão}}. The second temple was built by immigrants in the municipality of {{ill|Bastos|en|Bastos|WD=Q4868368|lt=Bastos|1=Bastos}} 18 years later. Most emigrants performed rituals of worshiping the gods with their families, and less often with their neighbors.<ref>{{cite journal |author = FRank Usarski, Rafael Shoji |title = Buddhism, Shinto and Japanese New Religions in Brazil |year = 2017 |journal = Handbook of Contemporary Religions in Brazil |pages = 279–294 |doi = 10.1163/9789004322134_018|isbn = 9789004322134 }}</ref> 11 major shrines are still located in Brazil.{{sfn|Picken|2011|p=274}}

== In Oceania ==

=== Micronesia ===
{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} dating from during or after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} exist in some {{ill|Micronesia|en|Micronesia|WD=Q3359409|lt=Micronesian|1=Micronesia}} countries.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oskow |first=Noah |date=2020-07-13 |title=When Japan Ruled the Waves: The Forgotten Colonies of Micronesia |url=https://unseen-japan.com/japan-forgotten-colonies-micronesia/ |access-date=2023-09-24 |website=Unseen Japan |language=en-US}}</ref>

== In Africa ==

=== Sub-Saharan Africa ===
Some Shinto-inspired new religions have gained ground in various cities in {{ill|Sub-Saharan Africa|en|Sub-Saharan Africa|WD=Q132959|lt=Sub-Saharan Africa|1=Sub-Saharan Africa}}.<ref>{{Citation |last=Louveau |first=Frédérique |title=Japanese Spiritualities in Africa: From a Transnational Space to the Creation of a Local Lifestyle |date=2020-07-06 |work=Transnational Religious Spaces |pages= |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |access-date=2024-01-25 |place=[[Munich]] |publisher=[[De Gruyter]] |language=en |doi=10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |isbn=978-3-11-069010-1}}</ref>

==References==
===Citations===
{{Reflist|20em}}

===Sources===

* {{cite book |last=Earhart |first=H. Byron |year=2004 |title=Japanese Religion: Unity and Diversity |edition=fourth |location=Belmont, CA |publisher=Wadsworth |isbn=978-0-534-17694-5 }}
* {{cite book |last=Hardacre |first=Helen |title=Shinto: A History |location=Oxford |publisher=Oxford University Press |year=2017 |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 }}
* {{cite journal |last = Nakajima |first = Michio |title = Shinto Deities that Crossed the Sea: Japan's "Overseas Shrines," 1868 to 1945 |year = 2010 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |doi = 10.18874/jjrs.37.1.2010.21-46|doi-access = free }}
* {{cite book |last=Picken |first=Stuart D. B. |author-link=Stuart D. B. Picken |title=Historical Dictionary of Shinto |edition=second |location=Lanham |publisher=Scarecrow Press |year=2011 |isbn=978-0-8108-7172-4 }}
* {{cite journal |last=Suga |first=Kōji |title=A Concept of "Overseas Shinto Shrines": A Pantheistic Attempt by Ogasawara Shōzō and Its Limitations |journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume=37 |issue=1 |year=2010 |pages=47–74 }}
{{Refend}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Overseas Shinto]]
[[File:Guji Francesco Brigante.jpg|thumb|A Shinto rite carried out at a jinja in {{ill|San Marino|en|San Marino|WD=Q238|lt=San Marino|1=San Marino}}, Southern Europe]]
'''Overseas Shinto''' designates the practice of the Japanese religion of [[Shinto]] outside Japan itself. Shinto has spread abroad by various methods, including the imperial expansion of the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}} during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the migration of Japanese to other countries, and the embrace of Shinto by various non-Japanese individuals.

{{lang|ja-Latn|Jinja}} outside Japan are termed {{lang|ja-Latn|kaigai jinja}} ("overseas shrines"), a term coined by {{ill|Ogasawara Shozo|ja|小笠原省三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ogasawara Shozo|1=Shōzō Ogasawara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q29410596}}.{{sfn|Suga|2010|p=48}} 

== In Europe ==
On June 22, 2014, Europe's first official Shinto Shrine: {{ill|San Marino Shrine|en|San Marino Shrine|WD=Q21192776|lt=San Marino Shrine|1=San Marino Shrine}} was opened in [[W:Serravalle, San Marino|Serravalle, San Marino]]. The San Marino shrine was inaugurated in the presence of 150 personalities including the president of the Association of Shinto Shrines and Yoko Kishi, mother of the former Prime Minister of Japan {{ill|Shinzo Abe|en|Shinzo Abe|WD=Q132345|lt=Shinzō Abe|1=Shinzo Abe}} and daughter of Prime Minister {{ill|Nobusuke Kishi|en|Nobusuke Kishi|WD=Q315572|lt=Nobusuke Kishi|1=Nobusuke Kishi}}.<ref>[https://giornalesm.com/266285/ San Marino. Sarà inaugurato questa mattina alle 10.30 al podere Lesignano, il San Marino Jinja]</ref><ref>{{cite web |lang = it |title=Un tempio shintoista a San Marino. E' il primo ufficiale in Europa|website=Il resto del Carlino |date=2014-06-22|url=https://www.ilrestodelcarlino.it/rimini/cronaca/2014/06/22/1082095-san-marino-tempio-shintoista.shtml}}</ref> The shrine was established in commemoration of the victims of the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|WD=Q36204|lt=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}.<ref>{{cite web |lang = it |url = https://st.ilsole24ore.com/art/notizie/2014-05-25/shintoisti-san-marino-081510.shtml |title = Shintoisti a San Marino |author = Stefano Carrer |date = 2014-05-25 |website = Il Sole 24 ORE |access-date = 2023-09-13}}</ref>

==In Asia==
[[File:Chosen Jingu.JPG|thumb|The [[:en:Chōsen Jingū|Chōsen Jingū]] in {{ill|Seoul|en|Seoul|WD=Q8684|lt=Seoul|1=Seoul}}, Korea, established during the Japanese occupation of the peninsula]]
In Japanese-controlled territories ({{ill|Wang Jingwei regime|en|Wang Jingwei regime|WD=Q696242|lt=China|1=Wang Jingwei regime}}, {{ill|Korea under Japanese rule|en|Korea under Japanese rule|WD=Q503585|lt=Korea|1=Korea under Japanese rule}}, the {{ill|South Seas Mandate|en|South Seas Mandate|WD=Q718763|lt=Pacific Islands|1=South Seas Mandate}}, [[:en:Singapore under Japanese rule|Singapore]], {{ill|Taiwan under Japanese rule|en|Taiwan under Japanese rule|WD=Q137816|lt=Taiwan|1=Taiwan under Japanese rule}}, {{ill|Manchukuo|en|Manchukuo|WD=Q30623|lt=Manchukuo|1=Manchukuo}} and {{ill|Karafuto Prefecture|en|Karafuto Prefecture|WD=Q696736|lt=Karafuto Prefecture|1=Karafuto Prefecture}}), shrines were erected by both Japanese settlers and colonial authorities. Most of the sanctuaries were built by settlers, but many of them later received government recognition and funding. At first, ''jinja'' built Japanese communities for their use, often using ritual objects from their old shrines.{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=22}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=30—32}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}} In 1900, the Japanese authorities erected the first large shrine in the colonies, it became [[Taiwan Grand Shrine]]. It was followed by other major shrines, such as {{ill|Chōsen Shrine|en|Chōsen Shrine|WD=Q488935|lt=Chōsen Shrine|1=Chōsen Shrine}} in Korea and {{ill|Karafuto Shrine|en|Karafuto Shrine|WD=Q11543654|lt=Karafuto Shrine|1=Karafuto Shrine}} on Sakhalin. These shrines were built by decree of the governor and without consultation with the Shinto clergy, and their policies were determined by the military.{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=404—406}}{{sfn|Hardacre|2017|pp=431—432}}{{sfn|Nakajima|2010|p=36}}

==In the Americas==

===United States===
The first Shinto shrine in Hawaii was {{ill|Hilo Daijingu|ja|ヒロ大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hilo Daijingu|qq=}}, built in 1898.<ref>{{cite journal |url = https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498 |author = Shimizu, Karli |title = Religion and Secularism in Overseas Shinto Shrines: A Case Study on Hilo Daijingū, 1898–1941 |year = 2019 |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |volume = 46 |issue = 1 |pages = 1–30 |jstor = 26854498 |issn = 0304-1042 |archivedate = 2021-05-04 |archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20210504095834/https://www.jstor.org/stable/26854498}}</ref> It is known that shortly before the {{ill|Attack on Pearl Harbor|en|Attack on Pearl Harbor|WD=Q52418|lt=Attack on Pearl Harbor|1=Attack on Pearl Harbor}} on December 7, 1941, Japanese Shintoists in Hawaii held ceremonies in honor of the former commander-in-chief of the Japanese fleet, [[Tōgō Heihachirō]]. One of the shrines, built at the beginning of the 20th century, was subordinate to both the Japanese authorities responsible for the shrine system and the American authorities, since it was registered in the state as a non-profit organization. American Shintoists were culturally influenced by their local environment. Thus, the spirits of {{ill|George Washington|en|George Washington|WD=Q23|lt=George Washington|1=George Washington}} and Hawaiian King {{ill|Kamehameha I|en|Kamehameha I|WD=Q214910|lt=Kamehameha I|1=Kamehameha I}} entered the local pantheon. After the attack on Pearl Harbor, the American government closed the shrine, fearing the growth of anti-American sentiment among the Japanese, but after the surrender of Japan in September 1945, the shrine continued its work. The few shrines on the West Coast of the United States were not restored after the war.<ref>{{cite journal |last = Hansen |first =Wilburn |title = Examining Prewar Togo Worship in Hawaii Toward Rethinking Hawaiian Shinto as a New Religion in America |year = 2010 |journal = Nova Religio |volume = 14 |number = 1 |pages = 67–92 |issn = 1092-6690 |doi = 10.1525/nr.2010.14.1.67}}</ref><ref name="Abe_Imamura2019">{{cite journal |author    = Abe DK, Imamura A |title = The destruction of Shinto shrines in Hawaii and the West Coast during World War II: the lingering effects of Pearl Harbor and Japanese-American internment |url   = https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816 |journal = Asian Anthropology  |year = 2019  |volume=18  |number= 4 |pages = 266–281 |doi = 10.1080/1683478X.2019.1592816}}</ref>

===Brazil===
Two Shinto shrines were built in Brazil before the outbreak of World War II, both in the {{ill|São Paulo (state)|en|São Paulo (state)|WD=Q175|lt=state of São Paulo|1=São Paulo (state)}}. The first one was built in 1920 by Uetsuka Shuhei in the city of {{ill|Promissão|en|Promissão|WD=Q431377|lt=Promissão|1=Promissão}}. The second temple was built by immigrants in the municipality of {{ill|Bastos|en|Bastos|WD=Q4868368|lt=Bastos|1=Bastos}} 18 years later. Most emigrants performed rituals of worshiping the gods with their families, and less often with their neighbors.<ref>{{cite journal |author = FRank Usarski, Rafael Shoji |title = Buddhism, Shinto and Japanese New Religions in Brazil |year = 2017 |journal = Handbook of Contemporary Religions in Brazil |pages = 279–294 |doi = 10.1163/9789004322134_018|isbn = 9789004322134 }}</ref> 11 major shrines are still located in Brazil.{{sfn|Picken|2011|p=274}}

== In Oceania ==

=== Micronesia ===
{{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrines|1=Shinto shrine}} dating from during or after {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}} exist in some {{ill|Micronesia|en|Micronesia|WD=Q3359409|lt=Micronesian|1=Micronesia}} countries.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Oskow |first=Noah |date=2020-07-13 |title=When Japan Ruled the Waves: The Forgotten Colonies of Micronesia |url=https://unseen-japan.com/japan-forgotten-colonies-micronesia/ |access-date=2023-09-24 |website=Unseen Japan |language=en-US}}</ref>

== In Africa ==

=== Sub-Saharan Africa ===
Some Shinto-inspired new religions have gained ground in various cities in {{ill|Sub-Saharan Africa|en|Sub-Saharan Africa|WD=Q132959|lt=Sub-Saharan Africa|1=Sub-Saharan Africa}}.<ref>{{Citation |last=Louveau |first=Frédérique |title=Japanese Spiritualities in Africa: From a Transnational Space to the Creation of a Local Lifestyle |date=2020-07-06 |work=Transnational Religious Spaces |pages= |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |access-date=2024-01-25 |place=[[Munich]] |publisher=[[De Gruyter]] |language=en |doi=10.1515/9783110690101-013/html |isbn=978-3-11-069010-1}}</ref>
